supply_chain.dependencies: China is the source of the majority of direct imports. Approximately 40% of Dollar Tree segment's total retail value purchases are imported directly, with a significant portion of goods purchased from domestic vendors also being imported.
supply_chain.risks: Disruptions from tariffs, duties, anti-dumping or countervailing duty orders, trade restrictions, geopolitical tensions, international disputes, military confrontation, economic sanctions, supply chain constraints, ocean shipping disruptions, port congestion, labor disagreements at ports, natural disasters, climate change impacts on distribution network

regulatory_risks: Product and food safety regulations (FDA oversight)
Labor and employment regulations (wage and hour, benefits, healthcare, workplace safety, OSHA rules)
Minimum wage increases by federal, state and local governments
Fair Labor Standards Act changes to exempt status salary thresholds
Privacy and information security regulations (data protection laws, PCI-DSS compliance)
Tariff and trade regulations (anti-dumping and countervailing duty orders)

Environmental regulations (hazardous waste, recycling, single-use plastics, chemicals of concern, extended producer responsibility, carbon pricing, product energy efficiency, animal welfare standards, product labeling)
Tax regulations and changes in corporate tax rates
Climate change-related government or industry actions
Compliance with DC 202 resolution requirements (internal controls, compliance codes, periodic DOJ reporting for three years)
SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program) eligibility requirements
Municipal regulations on store placement, proximity, labor relations, product types
DEI (Diversity, Equity and Inclusion) scrutiny and potential legal challenges
ESG (Environmental, Social and Governance) disclosure requirements

sanction_risks: U.S. tariffs imposed or threatened on China, Mexico, Canada and other countries in early 2025
Reciprocal tariffs announced by Trump administration
Retaliatory countermeasures by China and other countries subject to U.S. tariffs
Fees on Chinese shipping companies and Chinese-built vessels entering U.S. ports
Geopolitical tensions, international disputes, military confrontation, blockade, war, economic sanctions affecting international shipping
Red Sea tensions and Panama Canal traffic restrictions causing supply chain disruptions
